title: Modern Dining Table Pedestal
summary: A clean plywood pedestal base for a round or square dining top — four L-shaped legs that give a big table one central, leg-free footing.
description:
A pedestal base is what lets everyone at a round table actually tuck their chairs in — no corner legs to straddle. This one is deceptively simple: four L-shaped leg sets built from plywood, ganged around a center and tied with 1x4s, forming a sturdy column that carries a 48", 54", or 60" top.

The trick to a crisp result is the L-leg. Each leg is two plywood pieces joined at a right angle — one 16" wide, one 15-1/4" wide, so that when four of them wrap around the center the seams overlap cleanly and the outside faces stay flush. Get the rip widths right and the base looks like a solid turned column instead of four boards screwed together.

The top is a separate build (or buy) — this plan is only the base. How you attach it matters: mending plates work for a 1-1/2" thick top, but for a 3/4" top scrap plywood cleats are safer than plates that could blow through. And with any wide top, leave the attachment able to move a little seasonally, or a solid top will crack itself against a rigid base.

tools.note: The L-leg sets are joined with 3/4" pocket holes.
Clean, accurate rips are what make the L-legs wrap flush. A track saw is the alternative.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, matching veneer
materials.note: The whole base plus reinforcement scraps come from one sheet.

materials.name: 1x4, square edge, matching species
materials.note: Ties the four leg sets together at the ends.

materials.name: Mending plates
materials.note: For a 1-1/2" thick top. For a 3/4" top, use 3/4" plywood cleats instead.

cutList.note: 3/4" narrower so the L-legs wrap flush; rip one 16" strip down to make the fourth.
2"-wide plywood scraps to gusset the inside joints; cut to suit.

steps.title: Rip and cut the plywood
steps.body: Rip the plywood into two strips 16" wide and one strip 15-1/4" wide, then crosscut into eight pieces all 28-1/2" long. You need four 15-1/4" pieces, so rip one of the 16" pieces down to 15-1/4". Accurate rips here are what make the finished base look like a solid column.

steps.title: Build the four L-leg sets
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes along one edge of each 15-1/4" wide piece. Join each narrow piece to a 16" wide piece at a right angle to form an L — build four identical leg sets. Keep the outside faces flush; that's what hides the seam.

steps.title: Join the leg sets into the column
steps.body: Stand the four L-legs around the center so they wrap into a column, and tie them together at the ends with the 1x4s using 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Check the column is square and sits flat before the glue sets.

steps.title: Reinforce the joints
steps.body: Gusset the inside joints with the 2"-wide plywood scraps, glued and pinned with 1-1/4" brad nails. This is a big table's whole footing — the reinforcement is cheap insurance against racking.

steps.title: Add the top attachment and finish
steps.body:
Fasten mending plates across the top of the column to receive the tabletop. If your top is only 3/4" thick at the attachment points, use scrap 3/4" plywood cleats instead of plates so a screw can't blow through.

Sand and finish to match your top. The round or square top is a separate build; attach it so a wide solid top can still move seasonally without cracking.

images.alt: A minimalist plywood pedestal base supporting a round dining tabletop
